sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 21, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 20 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the maze structure more clearly:
- Rows 8-12, cols 13-24: all black (B) - the player's previous position (8,14)-(12,18) is now black, not gray. This is strange.
- Rows 14-18, cols 14-18: player (blue)
- Rows 14-18, cols 20-24: white (W)
- Rows 19-25, cols 32-38: white (W)
- Row 26, cols 20-38: white (W) - this connects the left and right white areas
Wait, looking at rows 19-26:
```
  19: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRB
  20: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRB
  21: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRB
  22: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRB
  23: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRB
  24: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRB
  25: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRB
  26: BBBBBBBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWBRB
```
Cols 13-42:
- Cols 13-24: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB (12 cells, all black)
- Cols 25-31: BBBBBBB (7 cells, all black)
- Cols 32-38: WWWWWWW (7 cells, white)
- Col 39: B (wall)
- Col 40: R (red)
- Col 41: B (wall)
Wait, but row 26 shows:
```
  26: BBBBBBBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWBRB
```
Cols 13-42:
- Cols 13-19: BBBBBBB (7 cells, black)
- Cols 20-38: WWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW (19 cells, white)
- Col 39: B (wall)
- Col 40: R (red)
- Col 41: B (wall)
So row 26 has a white corridor from cols 20-38, which connects the left white area (cols 20-24) to the right white area (cols 32-38).
But the player is at (14,14)-(18,18), and the white corridor at (14,20)-(18,24) is to the right. The player can't move RIGHT because col 19 is a wall.
The player can move UP to (8,14)-(12,18), but the cells at (8,14)-(12,18) are black (visited), not gray. This is strange.
Actually, looking at the ascii:
```
  8: BBBBBBBBBBBB
  9: BBBBBBBBBBBB
  10: BBBBBBBBBBBB
  11: BBBBBBBBBBBB
  12: BBBBBBBBBBBB
```
These are all black. So the cells at (8,14)-(12,18) are black, not gray. This means the player can't move UP to (8,14)-(12,18) because they're walls.
Wait, but the player previously moved UP from (14,14)-(18,18) to (8,14)-(12,18), and that worked. So the cells at (8,14)-(12,18) were not walls then.
I think the issue is that the player's movement "consumes" the path, and the consumed path becomes walls. When the player moved DOWN from (8,14)-(12,18) to (14,14)-(18,18), the cells at (8,14)-(12,18) became gray. Then the player moved UP from (14,14)-(18,18) to (8,14)-(12,18), and the cells at (14,14)-(18,18) became gray. Then the player moved DOWN from (8,14)-(12,18) to (14,14)-(18,18) again, and the cells at (8,14)-(12,18) became black (walls).
So the rule is: when the player moves through a cell, the cell becomes gray (visited). When the player moves away from the cell, the cell becomes black (wall). This "consumes" the path and prevents the player from using it again.
But wait, the player moved DOWN from (8,14)-(12,18) to (14,14)-(18,18) multiple times, and the cells at (8,14)-(12,18) became gray, then black. So the player can't move UP from (14,14)-(18,18) to (8,14)-(12,18) anymore because the cells are black (walls).
This explains why the player is stuck at (14,14)-(18,18). The cells at (8,14)-(12,18) are black (walls), and the cells at (14,20)-(18,24) are white but the path is blocked by walls at col 19.
So the player needs to use SPACE to reset the player to the start position and try a different path.
But wait, the player already tried SPACE and it reset the player to (8,14)-(12,18). Then the player moved DOWN to (14,14)-(18,18), and then RIGHT didn't work.
Actually, I think the issue is that SPACE doesn't reset the maze; it only resets the player's position. The maze state (which cells are walls and which are white) is preserved.
So the player needs to find a path from the start to the target that doesn't consume any paths that are needed for future movement. This is a "one-way path" puzzle.
Given the current state, the player is stuck at (14,14)-(18,18). The only option is to use SPACE to reset the player to the start position and try a different path.
But the player already tried SPACE, and the maze state was preserved. So the player needs to find a different path from the start.
Actually, let me re-examine the maze structure. The player starts at (8,14)-(12,18). The white corridors are:
- (8,20)-(12,24): white (unvisited)
- (14,20)-(18,24): white (visited)
- (14,32)-(38,38): white (unvisited)
- (26,20)-(38,38): white (unvisited)
The player needs to navigate from (8,14)-(12,18) to the target at (49,43)-(55,49).
The correct path is:
1. RIGHT from (8,14)-(12,18) to (8,20)-(12,24)
2. DOWN from (8,20)-(12,24) to (14,20)-(18,24)
3. RIGHT from (14,20)-(18,24) to (14,32)-(18,38)
4. DOWN from (14,32)-(18,38) to (20,32)-(24,38)
5. DOWN from (20,32)-(24,38) to (26,32)-(30,38)
6. ... continue DOWN to the target
But the player already moved DOWN from (8,14)-(12,18) to (14,14)-(18,18), which consumed the path. So the player needs to use SPACE to reset and try the correct path.
Let me use SPACE to reset the player to the start position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Use SPACE to reset the player to the start position
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
